speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: March 21st, 1995. Testimony by Andrew Gardner. Interviewed by Sandy Jacobson in Los Angeles, California, United States. The interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Sandy Jacobson. This is March 21st, 1995. We're interviewing Andrew Gardner in Los Angeles. We will be speaking in English. Andrew, thank you very much for participating in the project. Please tell me your name and the name you were born with.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name now is Andrew Gardner. I was born as [? Andrei ?] Gruenfeld in Hungary in 1925. I was the fourth of five children, five boys, and grew up as a young child in a very happy family, very orthodox. My father had a lumber yard in a small town where, in those days, he was equally liked by the few Jews who lived there and the many Gentiles. I remember as a child, in Hungary the winters were as cruel and cold as probably in the Midwest here. And we were also selling not only building material, but wood and coal for heating the homes. And sometimes, some of the women came and-- widows who did not have any money-- and came to ask for some heating material. And the foreman, the Gentile, said Mr. Gruenfeld, we are going broke if you keep on giving away the heating material for no money. But nevertheless, my father believed very strongly that where there is need, that's the overwhelming issue. And so it was-- however, I remember when the spring came, they really brought their first fruits to our house. Or if they had a stuffed goose or duck, they brought it as a payment. So we had nothing but good relationship with the population until about 1936, '37. But going back a little further, I remember I used to spend my vacation in my grandfather's house. My grandfather had 11 children. My mother was the oldest. And among a few others, I was my grandfather's-- one of the favorite grandchild. And I spent very pleasant memorable summers with them. My grandfather, not only was a very learned man in the Talmud, but he was, to that extent, that when there was a [NON-ENGLISH], a court case where the Jews did not want to go to the court of law, but they settled it among themselves, he was called as one of these judges. And in spite of it, he had the patience to sit down with his grandchildren and play all kinds of games. And he also had that rare ability that he was teaching us the Talmud in such a manner that we did not realize that we are learning. He was telling us stories, starting from Adam and Eve. And it-- later we realized, as we grew older, that they were actually the first introductions to our Talmud studies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in spite of it, that he was a very religious man, he was an ardent Zionist, which in those days, among the religious Jews was a no-no. He was almost criticized among his peers, that how can an orthodox religious Jew be a Zionist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in Palestine in those days, they knew that the Jews were not very religious. They were not keeping Shabbat. They were not eating kosher, and so on. But since 1933, especially when Hitler came to power, he had a foresight that something, something very bad could happen to the Jews. And again, as an exception, he not only let but even encouraged one of his sons and two of his daughters to go on [NON-ENGLISH] and then to go to Palestine. And all during the war, we knew that we have some connection to Palestine, even though we had no communication whatsoever with them. But I'm jumping from--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine. --periods to period. So-- Tell me a little bit about your childhood as far as your friends, and your going to school, and all of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since it was a small town, we did-- I did not go to a public school, but six of the children of my age, we had a private teacher who was teaching us all along. And at the end of the year, we went to the public school and we passed the examination, and this is how we advanced all the way up through the four elementary schools-- grades until we reached gymnasium, which is the junior high, the beginning of the junior high here. Then I enrolled in the gymnasium and went there for four years. At the end of the fourth year, which was in '36. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I believe it-- excuse me. Let me just go back for one second. '25, '31, and eighth year. '39. Excuse me, 1939 I stopped gonna school because by that time, one of my brothers, who had a nursery, flowers and plants, I took it over because I used to work with him in the summer. And from that time on, bad times started. First, one of my brothers, and then two of them, and then all three of them we called into forced labor camp. I do not know how much time should I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Take all the time you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Spend on this, but I think I skip it because this is where hard times started.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, tell us what you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940, they called my father to the forced labor camp, which at that time, we thought that was a tragedy of immense proportion. Whoever heard of a man of family should be called to a labor camp? Nevertheless, he went. But fortunately, six months later he came back. And then the war broke out with Russia. Germany invaded Russia, and they took my-- two of my brothers to the Russian front.

speaker: interviewer
text: As soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: As forced labor camp. In labor camps. And we lost contact with them. I did hear after the war that one of my brother was liberated by the Russians. But the Russians, instead of separating them from the regular soldiers, they force marched them to-- all the way to Siberia. During the way, more than half of them died, and then he never came home either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger brother I knew was not far from me in a forced labor camp. And he was also, I knew, liberated. And I heard the story after the war that right after the liberation, one of his friends fell into the typhoid fever. And he was carrying him on his shoulder, so to speak, to a hospital, and took care of him until he recovered. And then he fell into the same sickness. But this friend of his did not wait for him or took care of him, and he died there after the war. Which is again, another irony of fate that, you know, you survive and then you fell ill after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't get the name of the town you were born in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gyongyos, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is 60 miles southeast of Budapest. It is not far from the Matra mountains, with is a resort place, was and still is in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Can you describe the home you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The home, as I recall, was a very warm, caring, close-knit family home. And I do believe strongly that one of the reasons I survived was that I remembered and wanted to be back at least one more Friday night with my family. Friday nights were something, as I recall in the camp always, as a memory where table was covered with white tablecloth, the candles lit, my mother leaning over saying the blessing over the candles. After that, my father coming home from shul, making kiddish. And it was an interesting custom there in this small town that if there was a visitor in town, that everybody try to get him for the sabbath. And whenever it happened that my father brought one home, and unlike here now, that I would have to ask [LAUGHS] my wife before I could bring somebody home, there it was an accepted fact of life, whether my father came home with one or with three. It was all well and all right. And then it especially became festive because if it so happened that that person was also [NON-ENGLISH], they used to discuss the Talmud. And after dinner, this discussion went on and on. One story brought along another that reminded him on the third. And us small children just sitting there listening and finally [LAUGHS] almost falling asleep until our mother took us to sleep. So memories like this really gave us strength to hope and survive. And we did, some of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk a little bit about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was not only a very religious woman, but she was immensely well-read. Not only that, we had quite a sizable library of our own, but she went to the public library and read avidly. Current, ancient, there was no subject really that one could not talk to her about. This is not my impression only, but people that I met after the war told me. And she also had the ability that when we went to sleep, she started to read books, stories, from us, from Anderson, from Grimm, and all those fables. But later on, she started, when she felt that we're getting sleepy, she turned low the light and started to tell her own stories that she made up. And those stories actually I remember much longer than the ones that she read from the books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was a very charitable lady. And last sight I had of her was when they took us-- me out of the ghetto. I was on one side of the fence, she on the other, and I ran out of the line where we were put in to my mother. And a Gendarme kicked me on one side, and I saw my mother was dragged away from the other side of the fence. So this was the last of-- the sight I had of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to-- --inquire little bit about in your town, you had said that there were very few Jews. How many people lived in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to say that from Gyongyos, we moved to a small town, and this is where we had the lumber yard. This lumber yard we operated until 1941. When the Jewish laws came out that certain businesses could not be operated by Jews, because they were important for the people's well-being, that time they took it away from us. And he moved back to Gyongyos. And there, from 1941 to 1944, we lived there. And in-- in Gyongyos, there were some 1,200 Jewish souls. There was an orthodox shul. Our Rabbi was [NON-ENGLISH] whose niece is [NON-ENGLISH] in New York who started the Hineni movement. This is a movement where the [NON-ENGLISH] started to get involved with youth who were getting away from Yiddish guide. And she was very successful to bring many of them back. So that is about Gyongyos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you have friends who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the gymnasium, there were some 35 students in a class. And six of us were Jewish and the rest non-Jewish. And of course, intimate relationship developed with the Jewish kids much easier and longer-lasting than with the non-Jews, but we did have non-Jews. I was in the soccer club. We were playing football with them. We were in the gymnastics with them. So yes, there was quite an involved life. We went swimming with them in the swimming pool and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was just about to ask you about sports. So you would, you would attend your religious education and some-- the gymnasium, but you also then played sports.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. In those days, I was playing tennis, going ice skating, swimming. So until maybe 1942, we were really participating in most of these sports activities also.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about when were only 14 years old, you went to work in your brother's nursery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk a little bit about that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a rather responsible job for 14-year-old boy that was sort of dropped on me. There were some seven, eight workers whom I had to supervise. And also, this nursery was operated day and night. The wintertime, there were ovens that were heating hot water that was circulating in pipes throughout the greenhouse to keep the flowers from freezing. And that had to be right, because once it frozen, then the flowers would have died. And in the springtime, we had to take the flowers off to the market and sell them. So-- and we had some horses and wagon, and take them to the flower shops every so often. So it was an involved, rather responsible job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you take it over so young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because by that time, I was left to be the oldest from the children, because all the others were already in labor camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, those labor camps in those days were not as bad as later it became. They were let out sometimes to come home for a week or ten days. And we had some correspondence with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brothers have families?

speaker: interviewee
text: None of them were married before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So at, at 14 in 1939, you're working. You're a responsible young man working already. What inkling, what, what sense did you have of the war in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We already knew that the war is going on. We knew that on the Russian front, the situation was very bad, especially because-- at least we thought because of the cold conditions in this. And even more so, the Jewish forced labor parties because they had no proper clothing and they were exposed to the elements. And every once in a while came the news that one or another had died and was buried there. And that was the last we heard of them. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were forced labor camps operated by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Hungarians, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work was being done in these camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were digging [SIGHS] ditches for, I guess, tanks. They were preparing for the Hungarian soldiers lodgings. They were taking care of horses. All kinds of manual jobs that the soldiers were saved from doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your brothers had gone off to the forced labor camp, these Hungarian camps. And then your father was called. And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was called in '40, but he home after six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1940, He went.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- was-- how old would he have been? Can you remember about when he went to the forced labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost 50.

speaker: interviewer
text: Almost 50. So it must have been very grueling for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. This is was-- so difficult to bear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only that he was taken away at that age, but he was physically already-- you know, in those days, a 50-year-old man was an old man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. But he came back and he rejoined the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his health was all right at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when did the Germans come into Hungary? How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In March, 1944, they came into Hungary. And within a month, with the help of the Hungarian Gendarme and police, rounded up the Jews and forced them into ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to that, between the time your father came home in 1940 and 1944, what was your life like then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The atmosphere was already very tense. Our-- my brothers were already in the camps, so we really lived under a lot of strain and stress and worry. Life was not what it used to be, naturally. And we did-- In the early '40s and 41, we still had radios, and we listened to the BBC. And we knew more or less how the war proceeded. And we were hoping that, first of all, America did not enter the war then yet, [INAUDIBLE] and the moment they entered, we thought that's it, now it will be finished. Little did we know that it will still drag out for another three-- three and a half-- four years. So we lived from one day to the other in hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: You continued to work in the nursery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a younger brother still at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Imre.

speaker: interviewer
text: Imre. So-- and your mother and father, your father wasn't working?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was not working. My father actually, once they took away the lumber yard from him, he lost ground because that's what he did all his life. And he was not very adapt to take up something else. My mother, on the other hand, was very inventive. She started to organize a little seamstress school of sort in our home, where we took in work and she was teaching young Jewish girls how to sew. Because that was a time when intellectual jobs were not available. No-- Not even office work was allowed for Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she thought that by training them for a trade, they may be able to make a living. And in the meantime, she made some money with it too. So this is how she helped to keep us, us, the family going. She was a remarkable woman in that sense too, that if one thing did not work out, she was able to switch immediately to something else. So then that's how you survived from the beginning of the war until 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1944, from the ghetto, within three weeks, they took us out. We did not know then that this was actually a lifesaving type of an arrangement. There was a chief of police in Gyongyos, who, as it turned out after the war, knew what was going on with the Jews in Poland and in Russia. And he knew already when the ghetto was formed that very shortly they all will be taken to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in order to save some Jews, he took out some 200 people from the ghetto, among them myself, and told to the Germans that Hungary needs them to perform some work.

speaker: interviewee
text: First we started to work in some stables, cleaning after the horses in Gyongyos itself, and then they took us, finally the Germans took us to build some the airport for them in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to the point at which there was a ghetto. Did-- did your house-- was your house within the confines of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. For the ghetto, they selected the poorest section of the city where there-- there was a coal mine in Gyongyos and the miners lived in that part of town. And they used to live some 400 miners there, and they gathered 1,200 Jews in their place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they move the miners out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they put them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Wherever they emptied the Jewish houses. It was an even exchange. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they enclose the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it guarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, by the Hungarian Gendarme.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had the 1,200 Jews that lived in the town were all in this area that had, prior to that, been 400 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- how did they keep you together? Were you with another family?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were three families lived in one small house. One kitchen, one bathroom, and very miserable conditions. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was at least a roof over our head.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, it was your mother, and father, and your younger brother, Imre, and yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know the other families before they put you together with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we knew practically every Jewish family in Gyongyos. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like, I mean, having now to live, after having lived in your own beautiful home, now to have to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was already under terror. And we did not think, we just functioned, you know, from day to day. It was instinct that carried already, and the fear of tomorrow always. Every day, new announcements, new placards, you know, on the walls. So first--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the placards used to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews are not allowed to have more than 200 dollars-- 200 pengos in the Hungarian money, and whoever is found, Jews have to turn over their jewelry. Jews, they have any radios-- and every day they found something new that they could torment. It was a planned already well-rehearsed method of terrorizing and intimidating. We did not realize it then, but we know it now, and this is how they prepared the people that by the time they herded them to the wagons, they really went like sheeps. But in the meantime, when they took us out and put us into the forced labor camp, we were at that point still, fairly healthy and strong. They told us if one of you Jews tries to escape or sabotage, you know that we have your families. So for every Jew who escapes, 100 will be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they followed by then, to the letter, a well-established form that worked for them. Poland, Russia, many other countries. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about how did you get food in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some food distributed every morning, some bread. And besides, we carried in-- I was only four three weeks in the ghetto. So actually our reserve were still holding us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they did give, every morning, something to eat yet in the ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any organization in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not while I was there. Later I heard, yes, the Zionists, the [NON-ENGLISH] and others started to organize, but they did not have time because all together, by June, from April to June, they liquidated the ghetto completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, yeah. We didn't establish the dates. April of 1944 is when they did that. Was that right after Passover, right after Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was by edict? I mean, they came through the streets and announced--

speaker: interviewee
text: By edict, and then they came with these, again, on the wall, all kinds of notices and whatnot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- when you left your house, did you try to hide anything in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you successful?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did hide some silver, some gold pieces. And when we came back, all were gone of course, because they dug up everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewee
text: --now, you were taken out of the ghetto by the police chief, you said. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this when you recounted about having seen your mother for the last time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mhm.
